provenance.review_method: Model-assisted structural review of every supplied evidence note; no systematic full-text verification of the cited corpus

evidence.title: 2015 SHC014 chimeric SARS-like coronavirus (Menachery, Baric, Shi): documented gain-of-function capability at UNC/WIV
evidence.claim: In 2015, a Nature Medicine paper co-authored by the Wuhan Institute of Virology's Shi Zhengli (with UNC's Ralph Baric and lead author Vineet Menachery) reported building a lab chimera that placed the spike protein of a WIV-collected bat coronavirus (SHC014) onto a mouse-adapted SARS-CoV backbone, and showed the resulting virus replicated efficiently in primary human airway epithelial cells. The paper itself flagged that the work touched on the then-active U.S. gain-of-function (GoF) research funding pause.
evidence.evidence_quality: High. The primary source is a peer-reviewed paper in a top journal (Nature Medicine), with named authors including Shi Zhengli, and a stable DOI. The factual content — that this chimera was built and replicated in human airway cells — is uncontested by anyone, including Baric himself. This is documentary/virological evidence of *capability and collaboration*, not a finding inferred from contested data. It is independently corroborated by the 2016 PNAS follow-up and by detailed journalism (MIT Technology Review, 2021).
evidence.strongest_counterargument: The capability is real, but it is several steps removed from showing SARS-CoV-2 was engineered. Baric (the senior architect of this method) states plainly: "We never created a supervirus," that none of the SARS-like coronaviruses used were closely related to SARS-CoV-2, and that the published sequences are "very different from SARS-CoV-2." He says he personally believes SARS-CoV-2 is a natural pathogen and that there is "no strong and actionable data" that it was engineered (MIT Technology Review, 2021; Source 6). Critically: (a) SHC014 and WIV1 backbones/spikes are genetically far from SARS-CoV-2 — neither is a plausible direct progenitor; (b) the 2015 work used a SARS-CoV (not a SARS-CoV-2-type) backbone, a different lineage; (c) SARS-CoV-2 carries no signature (e.g., no known reverse-genetics scars matching this platform) tying it to this specific line of work. So the evidence establishes means and a track record, not the act. It is consistent with lab-leak but does not discriminate strongly between a natural spillover and an engineered/passaged virus.
evidence.confidence_assessment:
- **Claim confidence:** 0.98 — Every element of the core factual claim is verified against the peer-reviewed primary source and is uncontested. The only minor caveat is that "SHC014 collected by WIV" is best described as identified/sequenced by the Shi lab; the chimera-building was done at UNC using WIV-supplied sequences/reagents.
- **Support strength:** moderate (for lab-leak) — It robustly establishes that the exact institutions implicated (WIV + a close collaborator) had, and exercised, the capability to construct chimeric, human-cell-infecting SARS-like coronaviruses years before the pandemic, and that such work was deemed risky enough to brush against a federal pause. But it is capability/means evidence, not a smoking gun: the specific viruses are not SARS-CoV-2 progenitors, and the platform leaves no signature in SARS-CoV-2.
- **Net:** A calibrated reasoner should treat this as strong, near-certain proof of relevant engineering capability and intent at WIV/UNC — which raises the prior plausibility of a lab origin — while not mistaking it for evidence that SARS-CoV-2 itself was engineered or leaked.

citations.citation: Menachery VD, Yount BL Jr, Debbink K, et al. (2015). A SARS-like cluster of circulating bat coronaviruses shows potential for human emergence. *Nature Medicine* 21(12):1508–1513. DOI: 10.1038/nm.3985. https://www.nature.com/articles/nm.3985
PubMed record for Menachery et al. 2015 (authors, abstract, corrections). https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26552008/ ; Author Correction: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32661400/

Open-access PDF of the 2015 paper (UNC ORFeome repository), including the gain-of-function pause statement. https://www.med.unc.edu/orfeome/wp-content/uploads/sites/609/2018/03/a-sars-like-cluster-of-circulating-bat-coronaviruses-shows-potential-for-human-emergence.pdf

Menachery VD, Yount BL Jr, Sims AC, et al. (2016). SARS-like WIV1-CoV poised for human emergence. *PNAS* 113(11):3048–3053. DOI: 10.1073/pnas.1517719113. https://www.pnas.org/doi/abs/10.1073/pnas.1517719113

Jacobsen R. (2021). Did gain-of-function research create covid-19? We asked Ralph Baric. *MIT Technology Review*, July 26, 2021. https://www.technologyreview.com/2021/07/26/1030043/gain-of-function-research-coronavirus-ralph-baric-vaccines/

framing_assumptions: Demonstrated capability to construct a SARS-like chimera is treated as evidence relevant to a later event involving different actors, viruses, and records.
policy_sensitivities: Weight depends on whether general capability evidence is admitted and how collaboration links are translated into event probability.
coverage_gaps: No specific construct, precursor, experiment, or chain connects SHC014 work to SARS-CoV-2.
correlation_risks: Correlated with DEFUSE, WIV biosafety, and general gain-of-function capability arguments.
capability_limits: Assessing experimental virology requires checking methods, model systems, and replication in the cited studies.

evidential: Capability is documented; relevance to the specific origin event is indirect.
framing_conditional: A method's existence establishes possibility, not that it was used in the event under investigation.

notes: Keep method capability separate from actor opportunity and event evidence.
This is a substantive structural annotation of the supplied evidence note, not an independent finding that its factual claim is true.
